translation: O Prophet, when you [Muslims] divorce women, divorce them for [the commencement of] their waiting period and keep count of the waiting period, and fear Allah, your Lord. Do not turn them out of their [husbands'] houses, nor should they [themselves] leave [during that period] unless they are committing a clear immorality. And those are the limits [set by] Allah. And whoever transgresses the limits of Allah has certainly wronged himself. You know not; perhaps Allah will bring about after that a [different] matter
commentary:
# Surah At-Talaq: Ayah 1

## Ayah 1

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَطَلِّقُوهُنَّ لِعِدَّتِهِنَّ وَأَحْصُوا۟ ٱلْعِدَّةَ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ ۖ لَا تُخْرِجُوهُنَّ مِنۢ بُيُوتِهِنَّ وَلَا يَخْرُجْنَ إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِينَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ مُّبَيِّنَةٍ ۚ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُۥ ۚ لَا تَدْرِى لَعَلَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحْدِثُ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ أَمْرًا

We begin with Surah At-Talaq, the last two surahs of the series of Madani revelations that have been guiding us through the practical realities of community life. This surah and the one that follows, Surah At-Tahrim, both address the profound institution of marriage and the relationships between husband and wife. From Surah Al-Mulk onwards until the end of the mushaf, we encounter predominantly Makki Quran, making these final Madani passages particularly significant.

This surah connects deeply with what came before. In Surah At-Taghabun, Allah ﷻ planted a seed: the warning that your family, your spouse, your children can become an enemy to you. How could this be? How should we watch out for them? These questions were left deliberately open, though the same surah immediately softened the warning by teaching us that even if they are enemies, we must treat them with a unique ethic of forgiveness and mercy. Now, in Surah At-Talaq, Allah ﷻ addresses the painful reality that sometimes, despite our best efforts, marriages come to an end.

### The Reality of Divorce

The word talaq itself carries meaning that illuminates its nature. From the root italaq, it means to release, specifically the release of two things that can no longer be held together. The tension has become too great, and separation becomes inevitable. This same root is used for shooting an arrow or, in modern Arabic, firing a bullet—the moment when two things that were bound together must separate. This is the reality Allah ﷻ addresses with profound wisdom and mercy.

According to Ibn Mas'ud (RA) and others, this surah was revealed almost immediately after the divorce laws mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, answering questions that remained outstanding from that earlier revelation. Throughout the recent surahs, marital issues have been woven into discussions of community life and the struggle of the Ummah. We saw the matter of zihar in Surah Al-Mujadilah, and now we receive comprehensive guidance on divorce itself.

### An Unusual Opening

The opening of this surah is striking and demands our attention. Unlike the other Madani surahs that typically begin with praise of Allah ﷻ or "O you who believe," this surah opens with يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ—"O Prophet." Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly, both here and in the next surah, using a form that signals extraordinary importance.

But notice something remarkable: the ayah begins by addressing the Prophet ﷺ specifically—"O Prophet, listen carefully"—but then immediately shifts to the plural: إِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ—"when you all divorce women." This is one of the mercies embedded in the structure of revelation. By beginning with the Prophet ﷺ, Allah ﷻ signals to the entire community that this is a high-priority matter. It's as if Allah ﷻ is saying: "This is so important that I'm addressing you directly, O Prophet, and commanding you to make a real sermon out of this guidance."

The shift to plural immediately after shows that while the Prophet ﷺ is the direct addressee, the legislation applies to the entire believing community. Had this guidance begun with "O you who believe," it might have carried less weight in the hearts of the mothers of the believers. Instead, Allah ﷻ honors the Prophet ﷺ in the opening, then extends the ruling to everyone.

### When, Not If

Another profound detail lies in the use of إِذَا (when) rather than إِن (if). In Arabic, إِن introduces something conditional and possible, while إِذَا introduces something that will inevitably occur. By using "when you divorce," rather than "if you divorce," Allah ﷻ acknowledges a difficult truth: divorce is a reality of human life. It's not something that might happen occasionally—it's something that does happen and must be addressed. As sad, as difficult, as traumatic as divorce can be for husbands, wives, children, and extended families, it remains a social reality that requires divine guidance.

### Why Not Begin with "O You Who Believe"?

Why didn't Allah ﷻ simply say "O you who believe, when you divorce women, this is what you should do"? The direct address to the Prophet ﷺ serves multiple purposes. It elevates the priority of this ruling to the highest level. Everything the Prophet ﷺ conveys comes from Allah ﷻ, but this particular address emphasizes that this guidance requires extra serious attention. The community must understand that this isn't just another ruling—this is foundational to maintaining the health and integrity of Muslim society.

### Taqwa: The First Medicine

The dominant spiritual theme whenever divorce is discussed in the Quran is taqwa—consciousness of Allah ﷻ. From the beginning of divorce discussions in Surah Al-Baqarah through the rulings about 'iddah, financial obligations, and childcare arrangements, we find constant repetition: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ—"be conscious of Allah," "watch out for Him," "have taqwa of Him." This emphasis isn't mere repetition; there's no exaggeration in the Quran, only purposeful emphasis. If Allah ﷻ stresses taqwa so heavily in the context of divorce, it means this is precisely what disappears first when marriages deteriorate.

When relationships fall apart, consciousness of a higher authority evaporates. People stop thinking about Allah ﷻ and become consumed with their own anger, frustration, or rage toward their spouse. The hurt becomes all-consuming. Whether lying down, sitting, standing, or eating, a person going through divorce can think of nothing but their grievances. You can see it on their face—the constant mental rehearsal of wrongs, the inability to let go. This is why taqwa is mentioned repeatedly: it's the first line of treatment. Without consciousness of Allah ﷻ, without remembering His authority and judgment, none of the practical advice that follows will take root in the heart.

### Legislation for the Entire Community

When the Prophet ﷺ is told to prioritize a sermon about divorce guidance, the entire community receives a message: this education cannot wait until someone walks into a counseling session ready to divorce. The knowledge must already be there. The word إِذَا (when) also carries the nuance of preparation—just as "when you're about to recite Quran, seek refuge with Allah" means you seek refuge before beginning, this guidance means the community should already know these principles before crisis arrives.

### The Timing of Divorce

فَطَلِّقُوهُنَّ لِعِدَّتِهِنَّ—"divorce them for [the commencement of] their 'iddah." The phrase لِعِدَّتِهِنَّ refers to the period that actually counts, the time when the count truly begins. The general principle, without entering into detailed fiqh discussions, is that divorce should not be pronounced during a wife's period of menstruation. The word 'iddah comes from 'adad, meaning count or number, and specifically refers to when the counting actually matters or starts.

There's profound wisdom here. During menstruation, women experience أَذًى—a word the Quran uses that literally means "pain" or "hurt." We saw this word in Surah As-Saff when Prophet Musa (AS) asked his people, "Why do you cause me pain?" This same term describes what women experience during their monthly cycle. When any human being is in pain, they become more susceptible to irritability. They may say things they don't mean. Their emotions are heightened, their patience thinner.

Consider our own experience: when we're hungry, sick, exhausted, or in physical discomfort, our emotional regulation suffers. A parent might notice their child suddenly developing behavioral issues, becoming aggressive or tearful for no apparent reason—until they eat something, and normalcy returns. In states of physical pain or exhaustion, anger and fatigue can blur together. Harsh words spoken in such states may not reflect true feelings but temporary overwhelm.

### Wisdom in Waiting

Some companions (RA) went even further in their understanding: between two periods of menstruation, if husband and wife have been intimate, divorce should be delayed until after the next complete cycle. The wisdom is profound—what if she's pregnant? Pregnancy can transform everything, not just legally but psychologically. The realization "She's going to become a mother" or "What about my child?" can completely shift a person's perspective. New thoughts emerge: "How will the baby be raised? Can I really separate our family?" These considerations might lead a couple to reconcile, to find ways to work through their difficulties.

The relationship between husband and wife undergoes a complete metamorphosis with the arrival of children. Marriage without children and marriage with children are, in many ways, two different relationships entirely. The dynamics shift fundamentally. While this change brings immense beauty, it also requires adjustment. For husbands particularly, there can be an initial period of adaptation when attention naturally shifts to the infant's constant needs. Over time, if the relationship matures healthily, couples become parents together while maintaining their identity as husband and wife.

This balance is crucial. Some families make the mistake of becoming only parents, letting the husband-wife relationship atrophy. The household stays together solely because of the children, while the marriage itself grows hollow. This too can lead to eventual divorce. The marriage bond must be actively maintained alongside parental responsibilities.

### Maintaining Records and Consciousness

وَأَحْصُوا۟ ٱلْعِدَّةَ—"and keep count of the 'iddah." This directive to maintain careful records serves both legal and spiritual purposes. The practical aspect ensures proper observance of waiting periods. The spiritual aspect requires continued attention and mindfulness during an emotionally turbulent time.

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ—"And be conscious of Allah, your Master." Notice the choice of the word رَبّ —your Master, your Lord. While Allah is the fundamental term for our worship, Rabb is the fundamental term for authority and lordship. Our servitude, our status as slaves, belongs to our Rabb. In the midst of marital dissolution, when emotions run highest, this reminder becomes essential: you're forgetting that you're a slave to Allah ﷻ. You must place your feelings under His guidelines.

### The Weight of the Marriage Contract

The marriage contract is no light matter in Islam. Allah ﷻ calls it مِيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا—a strong, solemn covenant—in Surah An-Nisa. The word mithaq specifically indicates a contract where both parties fully understand what they're entering. This same term describes the covenant Allah ﷻ took from the prophets to deliver His message faithfully. It's the same word used for the primordial covenant we took with Allah ﷻ before coming to this world, when we testified to His lordship. That same weighty term applies to the marriage contract.

Whether those words of acceptance were spoken in Urdu, Arabic, English, or any other language, when you said "I accept," you entered into a mithaq ghaliz—a divine contract. Breaking that contract has specific laws and regulations that cannot be treated carelessly. You cannot toy with Allah's Book.

### The Seriousness of Divorce Pronouncements

In the time of the Prophet ﷺ, a companion once gave his wife divorce three times consecutively in a fit of anger. Although revocable divorce is limited to twice—meaning you can take it back twice—he said it three times, thinking perhaps it was final or hoping he could somehow reverse it. He came to the Prophet ﷺ asking if there was some penalty he could pay, some way to make those three count as one: "I didn't really mean it."

The Prophet's response ﷺ was swift and serious: "The Book of Allah is being toyed with while I am still among you." This wasn't a moment for finding loopholes or easy exits. The gravity of those words, once spoken, must be respected. The sanctity of Allah's legislation must be honored.

This doesn't mean Islam lacks mercy or flexibility—the detailed fiqh of divorce includes various circumstances and considerations. But it does mean we cannot approach these divine boundaries casually, as if they can be crossed and uncrossed at whim based on our emotional states. Allah's limits demand respect. His guidance exists precisely to protect us from the chaos of unrestrained emotion, to preserve dignity in the midst of heartbreak, and to maintain justice when passion might otherwise overwhelm reason.

The repetition of taqwa throughout this ayah and those that follow serves as a constant anchor: in your pain, remember Allah ﷻ; in your anger, remember Allah ﷻ; in your sense of injustice, remember Allah ﷻ. He is your Rabb, your Master, and His wisdom encompasses what your limited perspective cannot see in the moment.

translation: And when they have [nearly] fulfilled their term, either retain them according to acceptable terms or part with them according to acceptable terms. And bring to witness two just men from among you and establish the testimony for [the acceptance of] Allah. That is instructed to whoever should believe in Allah and the Last day. And whoever fears Allah - He will make for him a way out
commentary:
فَإِذَا بَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ فَارِقُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ وَأَشْهِدُوا۟ ذَوَىْ عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَـٰدَةَ لِلَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ يُوعَظُ بِهِۦ مَن كَانَ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مَخْرَجًا

### The Decision Point

فَإِذَا بَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ (When they complete their waiting period)—the period mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah is over. Now comes the decision: فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ (Hold them back in a decent way). If you're going to reconcile, then do so بِمَعْرُوفٍ—in a way that everyone knows to be decent.

The word مَعْرُوف  is powerful. It means there are standards that everybody knows are decent standards for how a husband treats his wife and how a wife treats her husband. You have to live by those universal standards, not your own personal ones.

What happens in failing or abusive marriages is that the husband develops his own standards of what is good and bad, and the wife has her own—sometimes delusional—standard of what is acceptable. "I don't think I disrespect you." Really? You don't think so? Ask anyone else. Don't ask me, ask anybody else if what you say is disrespectful. Ma'ruf means there are established, universally recognized standards of behavior. If you're going to hold them back, you must live up to those standards. You can't say, "Well, I have my own way of dealing with it."

### Separating with Dignity

أَوْ فَارِقُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ (Or part with them in a way that's decent). Have a respectable divorce. Surah Al-Baqarah even dedicates an ayah about giving gifts when you let your wife go. Remember that you'll stand before Allah ﷻ on Judgment Day anyway. You're dealing with a slave of Allah ﷻ, and you yourself are a slave of Allah ﷻ. The good and bad you do to each other—you're answerable to Him for it. That should drive the way you conduct your divorce.

The act of marriage is done with consciousness of Allah ﷻ, and the act of divorce with excellence (ihsan) should also be done with that same consciousness. This is where most people forget. When it comes to divorce, families get involved, friends get involved. The first thing to disappear is the awareness that Allah ﷻ has guidelines for this.

You know what happens? A husband and wife who are divorced can't even see each other, can't tolerate each other. "Don't even say her name to me"—that sort of thing. That's not ma'ruf. You parted ways. That just means you couldn't be husband and wife anymore. It doesn't mean she's not the mother of your children. It doesn't mean she's not a respectable human being, or he's not a respectable human being.

You see religious people, knowledgeable people—when you look at them you think, "MashaAllah, these people are serious"—they get divorced and their emails are full of profanity. You think, "Why are you studying Quran? Why are you learning this if this is your behavior?" There's no connection between what we learn and how we behave.

### Establishing Witnesses

وَأَشْهِدُوا۟ ذَوَىْ عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ (And make two people who possess fairness witnesses among you). This is if they're available. The idea is that the husband and wife don't just say, "He did divorce me" or "I'm not going back" without witnesses. Have a couple of witnesses—fair people. Not her friends who will automatically back her up, not his friends who will support him unnecessarily. Bring fair people into the conversation.

وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَـٰدَةَ لِلَّهِ (And establish the testimony for the sake of Allah). This is testimony like testimony in court. If you're called to testify that something happened, the people who witnessed it better stand up and say the right thing.

### The Test of True Faith

ذَٰلِكُمْ يُوعَظُ بِهِۦ مَن كَانَ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ (That is the counsel being given to whoever truly believes in Allah and the Last Day). We all claim to believe in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day, but Allah ﷻ is saying your real test of faith comes when, despite your biases, your hatred and your anger, you're able to stand by what's right. You're able to maintain your testimony with integrity.

### The Promise of a Way Out

وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مَخْرَجًا (Whoever maintains caution of Allah—Allah will make a way out for them). Notice the structure here. This is an if-then statement in classical Arabic style. يَتَّقِ  is in the conditional form, and يَجْعَل  follows as the result. It's saying: if you really do have taqwa, then—in that case—Allah ﷻ will make a way out for that person.

People going through divorce feel stuck. Who's going to help them now? They're all alone now. There's no way forward. Allah ﷻ says: maintain taqwa, and He will make a مَخْرَج —a way out, an exit.

translation: And will provide for him from where he does not expect. And whoever relies upon Allah - then He is sufficient for him. Indeed, Allah will accomplish His purpose. Allah has already set for everything a [decreed] extent
commentary:
وَيَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَحْتَسِبُ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَـٰلِغُ أَمْرِهِۦ ۚ قَدْ جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدْرًا

### Provision from Unexpected Sources

وَيَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَحْتَسِبُ (And He will provide for him from where he could not imagine). Notice that يَرْزُقْهُ  is also conditional, continuing the structure from the previous ayah. If you maintain taqwa, then Allah ﷻ will provide.

Risk (provision) isn't just money. In the context of divorce, your spouse—husband or wife—is a form of rizq for each other. That is something from Allah ﷻ. Rizq includes anything that when we don't have it, we feel its loss. Husband and wife absolutely fall into this category. You go through depression if you're in that position of loss.

Allah ﷻ says: whoever maintains taqwa, in that case He will provide them مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَحْتَسِبُ—from where he couldn't even imagine, from a place he couldn't have calculated. He couldn't have had any projections that provision would come from that direction.

Since this ayah opened in the context of divorce but the language is general, this becomes a universal principle. Taqwa of Allah ﷻ is a condition for finding a job, opening a business and making it successful, receiving any form of provision. Whoever has taqwa—Allah ﷻ will not only make a way out for them but will start providing them from directions they never anticipated.

### The Sufficiency of Tawakkul

وَمَن يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُۥٓ (And whoever places their trust in Allah—He is sufficient for him). This is the second fundamental principle that appears in matters of divorce: tawakkul—trust in Allah ﷻ. The first was taqwa, and the second is tawakkul.

Without trust in Allah ﷻ, you cannot be optimistic that things will improve. حَسْبُهُ  means He is enough. Allah ﷻ is sufficient. You don't need anyone or anything else. He alone is enough for the one who truly relies on Him.

### Allah's Will Shall Prevail

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَـٰلِغُ أَمْرِهِۦ (Indeed, Allah will accomplish His purpose). بَالِغ  comes from the same root as بَلَغَ (he reached). Allah ﷻ is the One who reaches His command, who fulfills His decree. Whatever He ﷻ has willed will come to pass. This is ultimate reassurance for anyone going through difficulty: Allah's plan will unfold exactly as He intends.

### Divine Measure for Everything

قَدْ جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدْرًا (Allah has set a measure for everything). The word قَدْر  means measure, decree, proportion. Everything has been given its proper measure by Allah ﷻ. Your hardship has a measure. Your waiting period has a measure. Your patience has a limit that He has set. Your relief has a timing that He has decreed.

This is profound comfort: nothing in your life is random or endless. Allah ﷻ has set a qadr—a measured portion—for absolutely everything. Your difficulty will not exceed what He has measured for you, and your relief will come exactly when He has decreed it should arrive.

translation: And those who no longer expect menstruation among your women - if you doubt, then their period is three months, and [also for] those who have not menstruated. And for those who are pregnant, their term is until they give birth. And whoever fears Allah - He will make for him of his matter ease
commentary:
وَٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى يَئِسْنَ مِنَ ٱلْمَحِيضِ مِن نِّسَآئِكُمْ إِنِ ٱرْتَبْتُمْ فَعِدَّتُهُنَّ ثَلَـٰثَةُ أَشْهُرٍ وَٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى لَمْ يَحِضْنَ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰتُ ٱلْأَحْمَالِ أَجَلُهُنَّ أَن يَضَعْنَ حَمْلَهُنَّ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ يُسْرًا

This ayah addresses exceptional biological situations regarding the iddah (waiting period). Allah ﷻ describes وَٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى يَئِسْنَ مِنَ ٱلْمَحِيضِ (those who have despaired of menstruation)—women of older age who have reached menopause. The standard iddah is calculated based on three menstrual cycles, but for women who no longer menstruate, this presents a question.

Allah ﷻ also mentions وَٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى لَمْ يَحِضْنَ (and those who have not menstruated). This refers to women of marriageable age who, for biological reasons, haven't yet begun their cycles. Some women experience irregularity—cycles once every six months, once a year, or once every two months. Not all of Allah's ﷻ creation follows the same pattern. Some women don't menstruate until they're 22, others begin at 18, others at 14. These variations are part of how Allah ﷻ created human beings differently.

The ruling is clear: إِنِ ٱرْتَبْتُمْ فَعِدَّتُهُنَّ ثَلَـٰثَةُ أَشْهُرٍ (if you are in doubt, their iddah is three months). If they fall into these exceptional categories, then the waiting period becomes three exact calendar months rather than three cycles.

Then Allah ﷻ addresses pregnant women: وَأُو۟لَـٰتُ ٱلْأَحْمَالِ أَجَلُهُنَّ أَن يَضَعْنَ حَمْلَهُنَّ (and for those who are pregnant, their term is until they give birth). The word أَحْمَال refers to loads or burdens being carried. Their deadline to leave the home is when they deliver their child.

There's profound wisdom here. When a woman is pregnant, she cannot be removed from the house. Her body is changing, her difficulties are increasing. If the man has even an ounce of heart left, he's going to witness this woman going through tremendous hardship—experiencing nausea, unable to eat properly—all while carrying his child. How long can he remain angry at her? How long can he maintain his distance while watching her sacrifice for the life they created together?

If he stays angry and hard-hearted even through this, then good for her—she should leave. What kind of man remains unmoved by a woman's suffering during pregnancy? But for most men with any conscience, this period provides an opportunity for the heart to soften, for mercy to return, for reconciliation to occur.

وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ يُسْرًا (And whoever is mindful of Allah, He will make for him ease in his matter). Out of His decision, Allah ﷻ will create ease. This isn't just ease in general—it's ease specifically in the difficult matters being discussed.

translation: That is the command of Allah, which He has sent down to you; and whoever fears Allah - He will remove for him his misdeeds and make great for him his reward
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ أَنزَلَهُۥٓ إِلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِۦ وَيُعْظِمْ لَهُۥٓ أَجْرًا

ذَٰلِكَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ (That is the command of Allah)—this emphatic statement reinforces everything mentioned. أَنزَلَهُۥٓ إِلَيْكُمْ (which He has sent down to you). These aren't human suggestions or cultural preferences; this is divine revelation.

وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِۦ (And whoever is mindful of Allah, He will remove from him his misdeeds). Notice the progression of benefits from taqwa in this surah. First, Allah ﷻ mentioned providing a way out and unexpected provision. Then He mentioned ease in decision-making. Now He mentions the ultimate benefit of taqwa: your sins will be forgiven.

While سَيِّـَٔات  refers to sins generally, in this specific context we can understand it includes the sins that occur between husband and wife. We are often most casual with family, and perhaps between husband and wife, we guard our words the least. The feelings that get hurt probably cut deepest between spouses. The room for committing wrongs is much wider in marriage than anywhere else—but so too is the room for excellence (ihsan).

The Prophet ﷺ said, "And I am the best of you to my family." This is an incredible statement and a tremendous Sunnah. Of all the people he dealt with—companions who would give their lives for him, leaders who sought his counsel, communities who needed his guidance—he was best to his family. Can you imagine? We prepare when guests come over. We clean the house, put on better clothes, cook special food. We change our behavior. Cousins are coming for Eid? We make elaborate preparations. A husband goes to the office and smiles at the secretary: "How's your morning?" "Good." "Great to hear!" He's pleasant to everyone except his wife.

He comes home: "How was your day?" "I don't want to talk about it. Long day." He would never speak that way to the secretary at the office, but he'll say it to his wife. These small sins pile up. When the Messenger ﷺ says he's the best to his family, that's not just a nice phrase to quote in lectures. What that actually means carries a very heavy obligation.

Allah ﷻ says whoever has taqwa, Allah will bury away his sins—He will cover them, erase them. وَيُعْظِمْ لَهُۥٓ أَجْرًا (and He will magnify for him his reward). Notice: not just give reward, but magnify it, make it great, expand it.

translation: Lodge them [in a section] of where you dwell out of your means and do not harm them in order to oppress them. And if they should be pregnant, then spend on them until they give birth. And if they breastfeed for you, then give them their payment and confer among yourselves in the acceptable way; but if you are in discord, then there may breastfeed for the father another woman
commentary:
أَسْكِنُوهُنَّ مِنْ حَيْثُ سَكَنتُم مِّن وُجْدِكُمْ وَلَا تُضَآرُّوهُنَّ لِتُضَيِّقُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ ۚ وَإِن كُنَّ أُو۟لَـٰتِ حَمْلٍ فَأَنفِقُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ حَتَّىٰ يَضَعْنَ حَمْلَهُنَّ ۚ فَإِنْ أَرْضَعْنَ لَكُمْ فَـَٔاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ ۖ وَأْتَمِرُوا۟ بَيْنَكُم بِمَعْرُوفٍ ۖ وَإِن تَعَاسَرْتُمْ فَسَتُرْضِعُ لَهُۥٓ أُخْرَىٰ

أَسْكِنُوهُنَّ مِنْ حَيْثُ سَكَنتُم (Lodge them where you lodge)—keep them stationed, keep them at home. The command is clear: from the same place where you live, from whatever you have available. Don't say, "You can go to the basement now that we're divorced. I'll throw a sheet down and you can sleep on the dirt floor because that bedroom is no longer for you."

مِّن وُجْدِكُمْ means from whatever is found with you, whatever you possess. Imagine a man who isn't wealthy—he doesn't have a three-bedroom house. He has a one-bedroom apartment. She's in her waiting period. One room is taken by the children. He doesn't have two beds; he has one bed. So now they have two blankets and they're sleeping back-to-back. How long before tensions ease? How long before one of them turns around? She asks, "Do you want some water or something?" "Sure."

This is the wisdom of Allah ﷻ: keep them where you are so that tensions naturally dissolve, so that proximity creates opportunities for hearts to soften.

وَلَا تُضَآرُّوهُنَّ لِتُضَيِّقُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ (And do not harm them in order to oppress them). The word تُضَيِّق means to make things tight for somebody, to squeeze them, to constrict their life. Some men are complete fools in this regard. They want to keep her during the iddah, but they want to make her life miserable before letting her go. They comply with the letter of the law—"She can stay where I stay"—but they ensure her life becomes so intolerable that after these months are done, she leaves on her own.

Then if somebody asks, "Why did you kick her out?" he can say, "No, no, she left on her own. Ask her. I didn't kick her out." But he made life so unbearable that she had no choice. Allah ﷻ says: don't play that game.

وَإِن كُنَّ أُو۟لَـٰتِ حَمْلٍ فَأَنفِقُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ حَتَّىٰ يَضَعْنَ حَمْلَهُنَّ (And if they should be pregnant, then spend on them until they give birth). If they're pregnant, you must continue spending on them until they deliver the child.

فَإِنْ أَرْضَعْنَ لَكُمْ فَـَٔاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ (Then if they breastfeed for you, give them their payment). This is remarkable. Once the child is delivered, the divorce is complete—you're no longer married. Now if she's going to feed your child, this is a service she's providing. Think about this naturally: a mother feeding her child isn't normally considered a favor to anyone else. It's an act of the mother to her child. She doesn't get paid for that. But Islam provided social security to divorced women to the point where even this most natural of acts requires compensation.

Biologically, women who give birth and don't feed their children experience pain—they need to feed. Yet Allah ﷻ says if she feeds your child, you must pay her. Why? Because that's your child and you're still responsible for his feeding. As head of the household, you're responsible for finances, which includes feeding. Even if food is coming from her body naturally, that doesn't eliminate the fact that this was your financial responsibility.

وَأْتَمِرُوا۟ بَيْنَكُم بِمَعْرُوفٍ (And conduct your affairs between yourselves in kindness). The word تَأْمِرُوا comes from أَمْر , which relates to reaching decisions. The form تَأَمَّرَ indicates back-and-forth negotiation before a decision is reached. When you're negotiating what she should be paid, she might come up with a ridiculous figure: "For every day I feed this child, I want $1,000. I want $2,000 per day." Or he might offer something equally absurd: "Okay, I'll give you a quarter every month. Twenty-five cents because I'm feeling generous. So for four months, I'll give you a whole shiny new dollar, crisp from the bank."

Allah ﷻ says conduct these negotiations بِمَعْرُوفٍ—with fairness, with what is recognized as reasonable and just.

وَإِن تَعَاسَرْتُمْ فَسَتُرْضِعُ لَهُۥٓ أُخْرَىٰ (But if you make difficulties for each other, then another woman will breastfeed for him). If negotiations break down, if you're giving each other a hard time and can't reach agreement, then outsource it. Find a wet nurse or somebody else. That's how it will have to be.

translation: Let a man of wealth spend from his wealth, and he whose provision is restricted - let him spend from what Allah has given him. Allah does not charge a soul except [according to] what He has given it. Allah will bring about, after hardship, ease
commentary:
لِيُنفِقْ ذُو سَعَةٍ مِّن سَعَتِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن قُدِرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقُهُۥ فَلْيُنفِقْ مِمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا مَآ ءَاتَىٰهَا ۚ سَيَجْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ بَعْدَ عُسْرٍ يُسْرًا

لِيُنفِقْ ذُو سَعَةٍ مِّن سَعَتِهِۦ (Let a man of wealth spend from his wealth). The one who has ample means should spend out of his abundance. Earlier in the surah, Allah ﷻ spoke about providing رِزْق without any calculation or audit—abundant, unrestricted provision.

وَمَن قُدِرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقُهُۥ فَلْيُنفِقْ مِمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ (But he whose provision is restricted, let him spend from what Allah has given him). The word قُدِرَ means to put a measure or limit on something. Here, Allah ﷻ describes someone whose rizq has been calculated, restricted, tightened. This person should spend from whatever Allah ﷻ has given him—not from what he doesn't have, but from what he has.

لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا مَآ ءَاتَىٰهَا (Allah does not burden a soul beyond what He has given it). This is one of the most comforting principles in all of Islamic law. Allah ﷻ doesn't burden anyone except according to what they've been given. You're only responsible for spending according to your capacity.

سَيَجْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ بَعْدَ عُسْرٍ يُسْرًا (Allah will bring about, after hardship, ease). This carries multiple meanings in the context of divorce. After the difficult period of separation is complete, after the financial strain, after the emotional hardship, Allah ﷻ will produce a time of ease. Allah ﷻ has promised this to those who get divorced among the believing community—if they maintain taqwa, their lives will become easier.

## Ayah 8-10

وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ عَتَتْ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهَا وَرُسُلِهِۦ فَحَاسَبْنَـٰهَا حِسَابًا شَدِيدًا وَعَذَّبْنَـٰهَا عَذَابًا نُّكْرًا

فَذَاقَتْ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهَا وَكَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ أَمْرِهَا خُسْرًا

أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۚ قَدْ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكُمْ ذِكْرًا

These three ayahs shift dramatically from family law to the destruction of nations. At first, this might seem jarring—why, in the middle of detailed divorce legislation, does Allah ﷻ suddenly discuss the annihilation of entire civilizations?

وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ عَتَتْ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهَا وَرُسُلِهِۦ (And how many a city was insolent toward the command of its Lord and His messengers). The word عَتَتْ means to be arrogant, to transgress out of pride. These towns didn't just disobey—they disobeyed arrogantly, defiantly, out of a sense of superiority.

فَحَاسَبْنَـٰهَا حِسَابًا شَدِيدًا (So We took it to account with a severe account). The word حِسَاب refers to an audit, a reckoning. Allah ﷻ brought them to account intensely. وَعَذَّبْنَـٰهَا عَذَابًا نُّكْرًا (and We punished it with a terrible punishment). نُكْر describes something unfamiliar, unknown, unrecognizable in its horror—a type of punishment they had never experienced before.

Why mention the destruction of nations in the middle of divorce laws? Because entire civilizations were annihilated for the very attitude being warned against here: arrogant disobedience. The words عَتَتْ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهَا (insolent toward the command of their Lord) echo throughout this passage. When you understand what's underlying these divorce regulations—the attitude required, the taqwa demanded, the accountability to Allah's ﷻ commands—you realize the connection. Nations were destroyed for the same arrogance you might be showing in your divorce process if you abandon taqwa and fail to follow these guidelines.

فَذَاقَتْ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهَا (So it tasted the bad consequence of its affair). وَبَال refers to consequences, particularly negative outcomes. They tasted the results of their own decisions. وَكَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ أَمْرِهَا خُسْرًا (And the outcome of its affair was loss)—total, complete, ultimate loss.

أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا (Allah has prepared for them a severe punishment). This punishment isn't just what they faced in this world—it continues into the Hereafter.

فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (So fear Allah, O you of understanding). يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ is a beautiful phrase in the Quran—people of sound minds, people who can think clearly. Allah ﷻ is saying: Do you realize what I just said? Have you given thought to this? I was just discussing divorce, and then I told you how I destroyed nations. Be careful who you're dealing with. Be careful about treating Allah's ﷻ commands lightly.

ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (those who have believed)—on top of having blessed intellects, you also have faith. قَدْ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكُمْ ذِكْرًا (Allah has sent down to you a reminder). This is Allah's ﷻ way of reminding you. The word ذِكْر  in the Quran refers to the Quran itself—but look at the eloquence. The reminder Allah ﷻ sent takes the form of what follows in the next ayah.

translation: And how many a city was insolent toward the command of its Lord and His messengers, so We took it to severe account and punished it with a terrible punishment
And it tasted the bad consequence of its affair, and the outcome of its affair was loss
Allah has prepared for them a severe punishment; so fear Allah, O you of understanding who have believed. Allah has sent down to you the Qur'an

[He sent] a Messenger [Muhammad] reciting to you the distinct verses of Allah that He may bring out those who believe and do righteous deeds from darknesses into the light. And whoever believes in Allah and does righteousness - He will admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow to abide therein forever. Allah will have perfected for him a provision
commentary:
رَّسُولًا يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ مُبَيِّنَـٰتٍ لِّيُخْرِجَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۚ وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ قَدْ أَحْسَنَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُۥ رِزْقًا

رَّسُولًا (A Messenger)—the reminder Allah ﷻ sent happens to be a Messenger. This teaches something critical about the Quran and the Prophet ﷺ: they are inseparable. You cannot separate the Quran from the Messenger ﷺ. The Quran came through him, was explained by him, was embodied by him.

يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ مُبَيِّنَـٰتٍ (reciting to you the verses of Allah, clear and clarifying). The word مُبَيِّنَـٰت combines two meanings: ayat that are abundantly clear in themselves, and ayat that clarify thoroughly, that make other things clear. These verses are both self-evident and illuminating.

This is what the surah began with—highlighting the Prophet ﷺ—and now it ends by highlighting the Prophet ﷺ again. The ayat of Allah ﷻ must be delivered through the Messenger ﷺ. You cannot undermine his role.

لِّيُخْرِجَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ (That he might bring out those who believe and do righteous deeds from darknesses into the light). Usually when Allah ﷻ mentions bringing people from darkness to light, He says simply "those who believe." Here, He adds وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ (and do righteous deeds). Why? Because having iman is essential, but you must also behave in the way this surah is asking you to behave. You already have iman—now act righteously, especially in these divorce matters. Then yes, Allah ﷻ will bring you out of darkness into light.

وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا (And whoever believes in Allah and does righteousness). Again, iman coupled with righteous action. يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ (He will admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow).

Notice something beautiful: يُدْخِلْهُ (He will admit him) is singular, but جَنَّـٰتٍ (gardens) is plural. Allah ﷻ welcomes you into your first garden and you're amazed: "Wow, this is incredible!" Then He says, "Wait, go through that gate to your next garden." Garden after garden after garden.

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا (abiding therein forever). قَدْ أَحْسَنَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُۥ رِزْقًا (Allah has certainly perfected for him provision). Allah ﷻ has definitely excelled, has done the ultimate ihsan in providing for him. You see, throughout this surah, Allah ﷻ mentioned rizq in this world—وَيَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَحْتَسِبُ (and He will provide for him from where he does not expect). But the ultimate rizq is in the Akhirah.

translation: It is Allah who has created seven heavens and of the earth, the like of them. [His] command descends among them so you may know that Allah is over all things competent and that Allah has encompassed all things in knowledge
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ وَمِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ مِثْلَهُنَّ يَتَنَزَّلُ ٱلْأَمْرُ بَيْنَهُنَّ لِتَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ أَحَاطَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًۢا

The surah concludes with a majestic declaration of Allah's ﷻ sovereignty over all creation.

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ (Allah is He who created seven heavens). وَمِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ مِثْلَهُنَّ (and of the earth, the like of them)—there are seven earths as well. This is from the unseen realities (ghayb) that we affirm through revelation.

يَتَنَزَّلُ ٱلْأَمْرُ بَيْنَهُنَّ (the command descends among them). The أَمْر of Allah ﷻ, His executive command, descends through these layers of creation.

لِتَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (so you may know that Allah is over all things competent). In situations of divorce, people often feel things are spiraling out of their control. They want to control outcomes: "Why aren't things working the way I want them to work?" This ayah reminds us that ultimate control belongs to Allah ﷻ alone. The أَمْر—the command, the decree—comes from Allah ﷻ, and He is in complete control over all things.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ أَحَاطَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًۢا (and that Allah has encompassed all things in knowledge). The word أَحَاطَ means to surround completely, to encircle, to envelope. Allah ﷻ has already encircled all things in terms of knowledge. Nothing escapes His knowledge—not your situation, not your pain, not your struggles, not your intentions, not what's hidden in your heart or your spouse's heart.

This is the perfect conclusion to a surah that began with precise regulations about divorce and expanded to encompass provision, taqwa, divine wisdom, the destruction of nations, the role of the Prophet ﷺ, and finally the absolute power and knowledge of Allah ﷻ. The message is clear: in your most difficult personal moments, when your family is falling apart, remember who is ultimately in control. Remember whose knowledge encompasses everything. Remember that the same One who created seven heavens and seven earths, whose command descends through all creation, is fully aware of your situation and will see you through if you maintain taqwa.
